description: Stryker is a global medical technology company focused on orthopaedics, surgical equipment, neurotechnology, and connected procedural platforms, with Mako robotics and Triathlon knee implants anchoring its joint-replacement franchise.

overview.title: Business Profile
paragraphs: Stryker sits in the large-cap medtech lane: not a drugmaker, not a hospital operator, and not exactly a neutral bystander in the economics of surgery. Its portfolio spans orthopaedic implants, surgical instruments, endoscopy, emergency-care equipment, neurovascular products, and related services sold primarily to hospitals and surgical providers.

The company reports through two major operating segments, MedSurg and Neurotechnology, and Orthopaedics. That split matters for the registry because Stryker combines conventional device manufacturing with increasingly software-assisted procedural systems, making the product footprint both physical and platform-like.

overview.title: Orthopaedics And Robotics
paragraphs: Mako robotic-arm assisted surgery is Stryker’s most visible technology platform. It supports preoperative planning and intraoperative guidance for joint replacement procedures, pairing capital equipment with Stryker implant systems and surgeon workflows.

The Triathlon knee system is a core implant family in Stryker’s knee reconstruction business. In registry terms, it should be tracked not just as a standalone implant line but as part of a larger bundled ecosystem involving planning software, robotic assistance, instruments, disposables, clinical training, and hospital procurement relationships.

overview.title: Free And Open Alternative Lens
paragraphs: Stryker’s products operate in heavily regulated clinical environments, so the relevant alternative landscape is not a simple open-source replacement story. The freer-world angle is more likely to involve open clinical evidence, interoperable surgical planning data, transparent implant registries, open standards for device integration, repairability, and hospital purchasing models that avoid unnecessary lock-in.

The company’s strength is precisely what makes scrutiny useful: integrated procedural systems can improve consistency and adoption, while also concentrating clinical workflow, data, training, and consumables around one vendor. The registry should separate legitimate medical-device quality constraints from ordinary platform control wearing a lab coat.
